cards.back: When the compiler or linker directly associates a function call with a specific function at compile time (also called static binding)
When the function call is resolved at runtime based on the actual type of the object (also called dynamic binding)

cards.text: Non-virtual functions use {{c1::early binding}}, while virtual functions use {{c2::late binding}}.

cards.front: What are the advantages of early binding over late binding?
cards.back:
• Faster execution (direct function call)
  • No runtime overhead
  • Can be optimized better by the compiler (inlining, etc.)

cards.front: What are the advantages of late binding over early binding?
cards.back:
• Enables runtime polymorphism
  • Allows base class pointers/references to call derived class functions
  • More flexible - works with types not known at compile time

cards.text: Early binding is also called {{c1::static binding}}, and late binding is also called {{c2::dynamic binding}}.

cards.question: Which function calls use early binding and which use late binding?
cards.answer:
nonVirtual() uses early binding (resolved at compile time to Base::nonVirtual)
virtualFunc() uses late binding (resolved at runtime to most-derived version)
cards.explanation: Non-virtual functions are resolved at compile time based on the pointer type. Virtual functions are resolved at runtime based on the actual object type.

cards.front: Why is late binding slower than early binding?
cards.back:
Late binding requires:
  • A runtime lookup in the vtable to find the correct function
  • An extra indirection through the vptr
  • Cannot be inlined by the compiler
This adds overhead compared to a direct function call.

cards.concept: When binding occurs for different function types
cards.explanation:
Early binding (compile-time):
  • Non-virtual member functions
  • Static functions
  • Global functions
  • Virtual functions called on objects (not pointers/references)
Late binding (runtime):
  • Virtual functions called through pointers or references

cards.text: Late binding allows the program to decide which function to call at {{c1::runtime}} based on the {{c2::actual type}} of the object, not the type of the pointer or reference.
